content:
- Ramki Gaddipati Digital Twin - Office Hours Structured Summary

- II. Overall Context of the Conversation/Call

- The meeting was a scheduled fortnightly check-in primarily focused on reviewing the health and progress of several Pixel development and support initiatives. Starting with an update on key development deliverables and API replacements (notably the Salesforce migration and associated backend integrations), the discussion covered delays, risks, and the reporting of blockers affecting effective QA and releases. A significant portion was devoted to examining customer support metrics, specifically a sharply elevated customer contact rate, ticket volumes, and quality of service and response. Ramki drove critical questioning around how project/QA metrics and customer support data are surfaced, emphasizing clarity and accountability in reporting. The dialogue anchored on the urgency of reducing major customer-impacting incidents, the need for product/engineering teams to intervene directly (bypassing standard escalation routes when necessary), and a call for collective ownership across all functions to resolve the most severe pain points rapidly. The session closed on strong instructions from Ramki to bypass formal processes and prioritize incident-driven fixes, with clear assignment of personal liability for the state of customer experience and tangible urgency around metrics.

- IV. Key Takeaways from the Call

- Salesforce Migration Scope Clarified: Backend API integrations are the only concern for migration; frontend changes are minimal and outside of current scope. (Ramki clarified intended boundaries and phased approach)
- Delay Attribution Needs Transparency: Project date slippages should be presented with explicit reasons/links to risk, not generalized; original vs. changed scope must be clearly demarcated in reporting. ("Risk is a probability thing, delay is a fact")
- End-to-End Testing Clarification: Delays in flow completion are due to both external (bank) and internal (unready APIs) blockers. End-to-end QA can work on deployed scope, but new features/extensions need phased tracking.
- Observability/SRE Implementation Scrutinized: Ramki challenges why SRE/observability work is taking so long, pushing for review/optimization and indicating he will personally review this plan.
- Metric-Driven Quality Culture Mandated: QA bug rates, dev leak per story, and customer-identified bug metrics are demanded as standard DLC/quality measures.
- CS Contact Rate Declared Emergency: 7–10%+ contact rate is “not acceptable,” declared as an emergency, and must be driven below 1%—with weekly resolution of at least two top customer problems as a commitment.
- Incident Response Model Prioritized Over Process: Ramki makes it explicit that in crisis, teams are to prioritize and act immediately on customer incidents, even if this circumvents normal escalation/bank approval processes.
- Root Cause Obligation: Teams are urged to categorize, quantify, and continually expose 90%+ of top customer friction points to all engineers/product owners rather than dilute/aggregate reporting—driven by empathy for the end user.
- Cost-of-Failure Spotlighted: Financial and reputational losses from persistent support issues are quantified, making clear the size of the “leak” and demanding product-side action regardless of “bug vs. enhancement” status.

- V. Ramki’s Insights

1. Boundary-Setting and Clarity on Development Scope:
- Ramki probes and sets boundaries around what is/isn’t included in Salesforce migration, clarifying that changes are predominantly backend and should exclude front-end unless later agreed. He insists on “scoping and understanding” before proceeding and is methodical in ensuring assumptions are shared with the team.

2. Demand for Factual, Actionable Reporting:
- Ramki repeatedly expresses dissatisfaction with vague or aggregated reporting of delays and risks. He demands explicit linkage of schedule slips to their origins, urging granular annotation on slides and pushing for a culture where risks are assigned and tracked transparently, emphasizing the difference between “risk” and actual “delay”.

3. QA and Release Discipline:
- He requests specific metrics on bugs per feature/story, demands a retrospective on high bug count in onboarding flows, and calls for standardization in QA reporting (predictability and quality metrics), arguing for parity between Pixel and other teams. He is insistent, pressing for data rather than narratives.

4. Customer Empathy and Incident-First Culture:
- With increasing frustration, Ramki labels the double-digit customer support contact rate as an “emergency”, emphasizing “you would never use a product with this kind of problem.” He calls for collective weekly commitment to solve top customer issues, pushing for genuine empathy and shared responsibility independent of formal processes.

5. Bypassing Process in Crisis:
- Ramki grants wide latitude to the team to prioritize, fix, and deploy customer-impacting solutions without waiting for full process sign-off or external alignment (e.g., from the bank product teams), taking personal accountability for any rule-breaking that might involve. His tone is unequivocal and urgent.

6. Financial Framing and Accountability:
- He contextualizes the ongoing service issues in terms of hard monetary losses (quantifying per support call, extrapolating to monthly spend, and team budget at risk), urging all stakeholders to see the direct link between product failures, operational costs, and user trust erosion. He speaks with conviction, seeking not just metric improvement but culture change.

- VI. Ramki's Suggested Action Items

1. Task: Categorize and expose top 90% volume-driving customer issues in granular detail for all engineers and product owners.
- Assigned To: Product and Engineering team leads (Lakshmanan, Kumaran, etc.)
- Ramki’s Instructions on Layout/Methodology: No summarization or distillation—full list to be circulated; "let every team member spend at least 30 minutes reviewing the real customer pain.”
- Timeline Mentioned by Ramki: Immediate, circulate as part of routine reporting.

2. Task: Mandate and implement weekly permanent resolution of the top 2 customer problems.
- Assigned To: All teams (Product, Engineering, Customer Success)
- Ramki’s Instructions on Layout/Methodology: Collective ownership; prioritize fixes regardless of formal process; escalate and act directly, Ramki will take responsibility for bypassing standard procedures.
- Timeline Mentioned by Ramki: Weekly cadence, starting now.

3. Task: Produce and share quality metrics—bugs per delivered story, dev leak per story, and QA leak (bank/customer-found) per story as DLC metrics.
- Assigned To: QA leads, Product Owners, Pristan Fernando
- Ramki’s Instructions on Layout/Methodology: Standardize with other teams (use existing templates); metrics to be part of every review/presentation.
- Timeline Mentioned by Ramki: Before next Office Hours/check-in.

4. Task: Organize a retrospective focused on onboarding flows with high bug count.
- Assigned To: QA & Product Teams, Apurva Jaiswal, Pristan Fernando
- Ramki’s Instructions on Layout/Methodology: Include breakdown of “bugs per story”, categorize root causes, and present findings to Ramki and relevant stakeholders in a designated forum.
- Timeline Mentioned by Ramki: Schedule in next office hours or a closely upcoming forum.

5. Task: Immediately represent project delivery risks/delays with explicit annotation of cause (use superscripts or footnotes tied directly to dates/slides).
- Assigned To: Reporting owners (slide preparers, Pink Floyd, etc.)
- Ramki’s Instructions on Layout/Methodology: Annotate all schedule shifts with precise, attributable reasons, linking each movement to either risk or a fact-based delay.
- Timeline Mentioned by Ramki: All future check-ins and presentations.

6. Task: Segregate and report all customer service (CS) metrics by pre-issuance and post-issuance drivers.
- Assigned To: Customer Service/Support analytics owners (Saravanan S, Pink Floyd team)
- Ramki’s Instructions on Layout/Methodology: Present data separately for each phase/product aspect; enable sharper diagnosis of friction points.
- Timeline Mentioned by Ramki: Incorporated into next reporting cycle.

- VII. Jira Ticket

Potential Jira Tickets:

1. Scope: Weekly Resolution Commitment—Identify and permanently resolve top 2 customer problems each week with full metric tracking and breakdown of volume drivers (including root causes, bug/enhancement flagging, intervention ownership).
- Assignee: Product & Engineering Leads (Lakshmanan, Kumaran, QA leads)
- Justification: Direct response to declared customer-impact emergency; action item from Office Hours with explicit CTO backing and personal accountability for bypassing standard blockers. Essential to reduce contact rate from emergency levels and restore operational defensibility.

2. Scope: Build, maintain, and report DLC quality metrics (dev leak, QA leak per story) as standard deliverable.
- Assignee: QA & Product Ownership (Pristan Fernando, QA analytics)
- Justification: To introduce standardized cross-team quality measurement, as mandated by Ramki, supporting continuous improvement and defensible reporting under CTO directive.

3. Scope: Full, granular, regularly-updated list of all top 90% volume-driving customer issues, in both pre-issuance and post-issuance phases, exposed and circulated across all teams.
- Assignee: Product Support Analytics (Customer Support analytics, Product Operations)
- Justification: Drives cross-functional situational awareness and prioritization per Ramki’s instructions; intended to increase empathy and direct action; enables construction of tactical solution plans.

- Note: Not all action items convert sensibly into Jira tickets (e.g., personal accountability, culture/process change); only those with clear deliverable scope and ownership are captured.

- VIII. Follow Up Questions/Suggestions

- Can the teams provide a granular, ticket-level breakdown of what constitutes each volume driver, flagged by root cause (e.g., process, known bug, documentation, product expectation mismatch)?
- What structural or systematic gaps are causing repeated issues to recur after an apparent fix—can we introduce regression watch metrics or specific product owner “sheriffs” for recurring ticket categories?
- What quick wins—documented by the CS/support ticket breakdown—can be executed within a weekly sprint that might provide outsized impact on customer experience (e.g., clearer in-app communications, self-serve FAQ, error message clarity)?
- Should a dedicated weekly incident triage and “blameless postmortem” session be instated as a space to dissect and own critical customer-facing issues, as an adjunct to standard QA/process reviews?
title: 2025-02-04_Pixel Check-in - Fortnightly (2)
